steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage concludes that the methodology was better explained in Vogel's study than in Richter-Klein's. The passage defines this through traceability scores — how clearly procedures are described. To weaken the conclusion, a choice must show Vogel's traceability was not higher.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘The study by Vogel had a lower traceability rating than the study by Richter-Klein and colleagues did.’: directly contradicts the conclusion — if Vogel scored lower on the metric that defines how well the methodology was explained, the conclusion fails. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘The study by Vogel had a high consistency rating and a high traceability rating.’: supports the conclusion rather than weakening it. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘The study by Vogel had a lower consistency rating than the study by Richter-Klein and colleagues did.’: neutral at best; doesn't clearly undermine the claim. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘The study by Richter-Klein and colleagues had a medium consistency rating and a medium traceability rating.’: mentions traceability, but gives only Richter-Klein's ratings; it does not compare Vogel's traceability to Richter-Klein's. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
